title: Soda farls
subtitle: Irish griddle soda bread cut into four quarters
excerpt: Soda farls are soda bread cooked flat on a griddle or heavy pan instead of in the oven. The dough is rolled into a round, marked into quarters, and cooked directly on the hob. They are a staple of the Northern Irish breakfast alongside eggs, bacon, and sausages.
sourceNotes: Traditional Northern Irish bread, central to the Ulster Fry. Soda farls are a form of soda bread cooked on a griddle rather than baked, giving a flat bread with a soft interior and a slightly dry, floury outside. The word 'farl' comes from the Scots Gaelic 'fardel', meaning a fourth part.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked farls for up to 2 months. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten fresh. Leftovers can be split and toasted.

content.text: Soda farls are very quick to make: the dough comes together in minutes with no yeast and no proving. The buttermilk reacts with the bicarbonate of soda to leaven the bread as it cooks. The dough should be soft but not sticky. Work it as little as possible: overworked soda bread becomes tough.

Mix the flour, bicarbonate of soda, and salt in a bowl. Make a well in the centre and pour in the buttermilk. Mix quickly with a knife or fork until a soft dough just comes together. Do not overwork it.
Turn onto a floured surface and pat gently into a round about 1 cm thick. Cut through the middle in both directions to make 4 equal quarters (farls).

Heat a heavy frying pan or griddle over a medium heat. Dust lightly with flour. Cook the farls for 8 to 10 minutes per side, turning once, until they have risen slightly, the outside is dry and floury, and a hollow sound comes when you tap the base.
Split and eat warm with butter.

items.symptom: The outside is done but the inside is raw
items.cause: Heat too high, or the farls were too thick.
items.fix: Keep the heat at medium and flatten the farls to about 1 cm. They need steady gentle heat to cook through.

items.symptom: The dough is too sticky to handle
items.cause: Too much buttermilk, or the flour was not weighed.
items.fix: Add flour a tablespoon at a time until the dough just stops sticking. A slightly sticky dough is better than a dry one.

items.symptom: The farls are dense and doughy
items.cause: Dough was overworked or bicarbonate of soda was old.
items.fix: Mix just enough to bring the dough together. Check that the bicarbonate of soda is within its use-by date.

content.text: Soda farls are a central part of the Ulster Fry, the Northern Irish full breakfast, where they sit alongside potato bread, eggs, bacon, and sausages. They are made throughout Northern Ireland and the border counties of the Republic. The word 'farl' means a fourth part, which is simply a description of how the round is cut. Like all soda breads, they are quick, cheap, and practical: no yeast, no oven, no waiting.
